summary.overview: Indiana energy affordability is under significant pressure as major utilities including Duke Energy Indiana, AES Indiana, and NIPSCO pursue rate increases tied to infrastructure investments, clean energy transitions, and rising fuel costs. Low-income households face compounding challenges from higher bills, inadequate LIHEAP funding, and complex disconnection rules. Advocates and the Office of Utility Consumer Counselor are actively intervening in IURC proceedings to push back on rate hikes and seek stronger consumer protections.

messaging_recommendations.message: Rising utility rates are forcing Indiana families to choose between heat and food — the state must strengthen low-income protections and scrutinize rate case requests that shift costs onto those least able to pay.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Framing the issue around concrete household tradeoffs appeals to both fiscal responsibility and constituent welfare, making it harder for policymakers to dismiss consumer concerns as special-interest advocacy.

messaging_recommendations.message: You have a right to affordable energy and protection from unfair disconnection — free legal and financial assistance resources exist, and your voice in utility proceedings matters.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Empowerment-based messaging grounded in rights language reduces fatalism and increases engagement with available assistance programs and public comment opportunities.

messaging_recommendations.message: Indiana utility companies are requesting some of the largest rate increases in decades while low-income households already spend a disproportionate share of their income on energy — accountability and transparency in the rate-setting process protects everyone.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Connecting individual household impact to broader systemic accountability broadens the coalition beyond low-income advocates and frames utility oversight as a universal public interest issue.

messaging_recommendations.message: Coordinated outreach and IURC testimony from community organizations amplifies the consumer record in rate cases and can directly influence commission decisions on affordability conditions and assistance programs.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Demonstrating that organized participation produces tangible outcomes incentivizes institutional engagement and builds durable advocacy capacity across the state.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana filed for a general rate increase seeking approximately $271 million in additional annual revenue, citing investments in generation, grid modernization, and rising operational costs. The case involves extensive intervenor testimony from the OUCC and consumer groups.

AES Indiana (formerly IPL) requested a general rate increase to recover costs associated with its integrated resource plan, including the transition away from coal and investment in new generation resources. The OUCC recommended a significantly lower increase than requested.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approved increase of approximately 15–17% for typical residential customers

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed a gas base rate case seeking increased revenue to recover costs for pipeline infrastructure upgrades and system modernization across its northern Indiana service territory. Consumer advocates raised concerns about the impact on low-income customers.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately 16% increase for typical residential gas customers

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed an electric rate case seeking additional revenue tied to ongoing renewable energy transition investments and transmission cost recovery under its long-term integrated resource plan. The case remains under IURC review with active OUCC intervention.

CenterPoint Energy Indiana pursued recovery of gas infrastructure modernization costs and pipeline safety investments through a rate adjustment mechanism, affecting residential gas customers across its southern Indiana territory.
rate_cases.rate_change: Incremental adjustment; estimated 5–8% impact on typical residential bills

articles.headline: Duke Energy Indiana Rate Hike Could Cost Typical Customer Hundreds More Per Year
articles.summary: Duke Energy Indiana's pending rate case before the IURC could result in residential customers paying significantly more annually if approved as filed. Consumer advocates and the OUCC have intervened, arguing the company has not adequately justified the full amount of the increase. The case is expected to reach a final order in mid-2024.

articles.headline: Indiana Utility Regulator Approves AES Indiana Rate Increase Over Consumer Objections
articles.summary: The IURC approved a rate increase for AES Indiana despite testimony from the Office of Utility Consumer Counselor recommending a lower figure. Low-income advocates said the decision will deepen energy burden for Indianapolis-area households already struggling with affordability. The commission did require AES Indiana to maintain and expand its low-income assistance programs as a condition of approval.

articles.headline: LIHEAP Funding Falls Short as Indiana Energy Bills Rise
articles.summary: Federal LIHEAP assistance for Indiana households has not kept pace with rising utility costs, leaving community action agencies scrambling to stretch limited dollars further. Indiana's community action network reports turning away eligible families due to funding exhaustion earlier in the heating season than in prior years. Advocates are calling on the state legislature to appropriate additional emergency energy assistance funds.

articles.headline: NIPSCO Customers Face Double-Digit Rate Increases Amid Clean Energy Transition
articles.summary: NIPSCO customers in northern Indiana are absorbing rate increases tied to the utility's multi-billion-dollar shift from coal to renewable energy sources. Critics argue the transition costs are being borne disproportionately by residential ratepayers rather than shareholders. The Citizens Action Coalition has called for stronger low-income protections to be embedded in NIPSCO's pending electric rate case.

articles.headline: Indiana Disconnection Moratorium Debates Return as Winter Ends
articles.summary: Consumer advocates are renewing calls for Indiana to adopt stronger statutory protections against utility disconnection for vulnerable customers, particularly as winter moratorium periods expire. Unlike some neighboring states, Indiana does not have a blanket winter shutoff prohibition for all residential customers, relying instead on utility tariff provisions. The IURC held a stakeholder workshop in early 2024 to examine existing disconnection rules.

articles.headline: Citizens Action Coalition Challenges Duke Energy Rate Case, Cites Affordability Crisis
articles.summary: The Citizens Action Coalition of Indiana filed detailed intervenor testimony in Duke Energy's general rate case arguing the company's revenue request is inflated and would impose an undue burden on low-income ratepayers. The organization is calling on the IURC to require Duke to expand its low-income bill assistance program as a condition of any approved increase. The case is seen as a major test of how the commission weighs affordability concerns against utility investment recovery.

articles.headline: Indiana OUCC Urges Caution on Utility Rate Requests, Points to Household Strain
articles.summary: The Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor released a report highlighting that a growing share of Indiana households spend more than 6% of income on energy — a threshold widely used to define high energy burden. The OUCC warned that multiple simultaneous rate cases from major utilities risk compounding the affordability problem for moderate- and low-income families. The agency is actively intervening in all pending major rate cases to seek lower approved amounts.

articles.headline: Indiana Legislature Considers Bills to Reform IURC Process and Consumer Protections
articles.summary: Several bills introduced in the 2024 Indiana General Assembly would modify how the IURC evaluates utility rate cases, including proposals to strengthen consumer advocacy funding and extend disconnection protections. Utility companies have opposed some measures, arguing they would create regulatory uncertainty and slow infrastructure investment. Advocates say the reforms are necessary given the pace of rate increases hitting Indiana households.

articles.headline: Energy Burden Study Finds Indiana Low-Income Households Among Most Strained in Midwest
articles.summary: A new study from a regional energy policy center found that low-income Indiana households spend on average more than 10% of household income on energy costs, placing Indiana among the more burdened states in the Midwest. The research points to older housing stock, reliance on natural gas for heating, and a series of recent rate increases as key drivers. The authors recommend expanded weatherization funding and stronger utility low-income program requirements.

stakeholders.name: William Fine
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: (Paraphrased) Indiana families are facing utility rate increases that are growing faster than their incomes, and the OUCC is committed to challenging rate requests that are not adequately justified and to pushing utilities to do more for low-income customers in every rate case.
stakeholders.source: OUCC press release and testimony, Indiana Economic Digest

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: (Paraphrased) Duke Energy is asking Hoosiers to pay more while the company continues to deliver returns to Wall Street investors. The IURC must weigh the real and growing hardship these rates impose on working families and people on fixed incomes before rubber-stamping another massive increase.
stakeholders.source: Citizens Action Coalition intervenor testimony and press statement, Environmental Law & Policy Center News

stakeholders.name: Jennifer Washburn
stakeholders.title: State Director
stakeholders.quote: (Paraphrased) Older Hoosiers living on Social Security and fixed pensions are particularly vulnerable to energy price spikes. AARP Indiana is urging the commission to require utilities to strengthen low-income and senior assistance programs before any rate increase takes effect.
stakeholders.source: AARP Indiana public comment and advocacy materials, Indiana Capital Chronicle

stakeholders.name: Stan Pinegar
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: (Paraphrased) The investments we are requesting recovery for will modernize our grid, improve reliability for customers, and support Indiana's economic competitiveness. We believe the rate case reflects a fair balance between necessary capital investment and customer impact.
stakeholders.source: Duke Energy Indiana press release and IURC testimony

stakeholders.name: Ahmad Qatato
stakeholders.title: President and CEO
stakeholders.quote: (Paraphrased) AES Indiana is committed to a responsible clean energy transition that improves long-term affordability. The rate increase reflects real costs of retiring aging coal plants and building new generation that will serve customers for decades.
stakeholders.source: AES Indiana press statement, Indianapolis Star

stakeholders.name: Violet Sistovaris
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: (Paraphrased) Our long-term transition plan is one of the most ambitious in the region, and we recognize customers feel the impact in their bills. We are working to include robust assistance programs and phased implementation to minimize hardship for those who need it most.
stakeholders.source: NIPSCO press release and rate case testimony, NWI Times

stakeholders.name: Ed Soliday
stakeholders.title: State Representative, Chair of House Utilities Committee
stakeholders.quote: (Paraphrased) We want to make sure the regulatory process is working as intended — that utilities can invest in their systems while customers are not being asked to pay more than is reasonable. That balance is what the committee is examining this session.
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle, committee hearing coverage

stakeholders.name: David Ober
stakeholders.title: State Senator, Senate Utilities Committee
stakeholders.quote: (Paraphrased) I hear from constituents across the district who are alarmed at the size of recent utility bills. The legislature needs to look seriously at whether current law gives the IURC the right tools to protect consumers in an era of rapid utility investment and rate increases.
